points: A force is a push or pull on an object resulting from interaction with another object. The SI unit of force is the newton (N). Forces always require interaction between at least two objects.

Forces can produce multiple effects: making a stationary object move, changing the speed of a moving object (acceleration or deceleration), changing the direction of motion, or changing the shape of an object. None of these changes occur without the action of force.
Whenever a change in speed, direction, or shape occurs, a force is acting on the object. Conversely, if an object is at rest, it means the forces acting on it are balanced (cancelling each other).

Contact forces require physical contact between objects. Muscular force results from muscle contraction in living organisms and enables movement and survival. Friction arises when an object moves or tries to move over another surface, always acting opposite to the direction of motion and depending on surface roughness.

Non-contact forces act without physical contact across distances. Magnetic force is exerted by magnets on other magnets or magnetic materials (like poles repel, unlike poles attract). Electrostatic force results from static charges built up by rubbing materials; similarly charged objects repel while oppositely charged objects attract.

Gravitational force is the force with which Earth attracts all objects towards itself—always attractive and acting from a distance. This explains why all thrown objects eventually fall and why objects have weight. Weight is the gravitational force on an object measured in newtons, while mass (measured in kg) is the amount of matter and remains constant everywhere.

Weight is the gravitational force on an object and varies with location and planet. A spring balance measures weight by the stretching of its spring when an object hangs from it. To read a spring balance, identify the range, calculate the value of smallest divisions, and read carefully.

When an object is placed in a liquid, two forces act: gravitational force downward and buoyant force (upthrust) upward. If weight > buoyant force, the object sinks; if weight = buoyant force, it floats. Archimedes' Principle states that buoyant force equals the weight of the liquid displaced.

The difference between mass and weight is crucial: mass is the amount of matter (constant everywhere, measured in kg), while weight is gravitational force (variable, measured in N). On different planets, an object's weight changes but its mass remains the same (e.g., 1 kg object weighs 10 N on Earth but only 1.6 N on the Moon).
